page: Amazon Web Services m3.xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine instance belonging to the legacy m3 server family. It is powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor utilizing the x86_64 architecture. The instance provides 4 vCPUs mapped to 2 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled, running at a clock speed of 2.5 GHz. It features 15.0 GB of system memory, yielding 3.75 GB of RAM per core, and includes 80 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. The network interface delivers a baseline bandwidth of 1 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks position this legacy instance in the lower tiers of modern cloud compute resources. Multi-core and single-core CPU tests, such as stress-ng and Geekbench, consistently rank in the bottom 10% to 25% of tested hardware. Memory bandwidth benchmarks also fall into the bottom 25%, although the PassMark memory score achieves an average rating. Cryptographic performance is supported by hardware-accelerated AES instructions, but overall compute-heavy tasks like software compilation and ray tracing show weak performance.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the m3.xlarge represents an older hardware generation with lower resource density and performance-per-resource ratios compared to modern alternatives. It is best suited for legacy workloads, basic web serving, lightweight database operations, or general-purpose utility tasks that do not require high-performance compute or modern instruction sets like AVX-512.
description: Amazon Web Services m3.xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring 4 vCPUs and 15.0 GB of RAM, powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor running at 2.5 GHz. Built on the x86_64 architecture and managed via the Xen hypervisor, this instance includes 80 GB of local NVMe SSD storage and a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps. Benchmark results place its CPU and memory performance primarily in the bottom 10% to 25%, indicating a legacy hardware profile with lower compute efficiency. While it supports basic hardware acceleration like AES and AVX, it lacks modern instruction sets such as AVX-512. The m3.xlarge is best suited for low-intensity workloads, basic web serving, legacy applications, and lightweight database operations that do not demand high-performance computing resources.
og_description: Amazon Web Services m3.xlarge is a general-purpose legacy instance featuring 4 vCPUs, 15.0 GB RAM, and 80 GB of local NVMe SSD storage, powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services m3.xlarge is a general-purpose instance featuring 4 vCPUs, 15.0 GB RAM, and 80 GB NVMe SSD storage on an Intel Xeon processor.
tagline: A legacy general-purpose instance with dedicated Intel Xeon processors, balanced memory, and local NVMe SSD storage for basic workloads.
bullet_points: Intel Xeon E5-2670 v2 processor with 4 vCPUs and 15.0 GB RAM
80 GB of local NVMe SSD storage included
Xen hypervisor virtualization on x86_64 architecture
Best suited for legacy applications and lightweight web serving
